CER01A.blurb:
LA's third Opening Ceremony — after 1932 and 1984 — unfolds beneath the translucent canopy of SoFi Stadium, reborn as "2028 Stadium" for the occasion. Two hundred nations parade into the California evening while the Hollywood Hills frame the backdrop.

The city that invented the modern entertainment industry gets to stage the world's grandest spectacle. The cauldron lighting alone will be talked about for decades — and given that this is LA, the list of people who could light it is longer and more star-studded than anywhere else in the world.

CER02.blurb:
The Coliseum becomes the only venue in Olympic history to host three Closing Ceremonies. The 1984 cauldron still stands as a monument to LA's Olympic legacy, and this is where Brisbane 2032 receives the flame.

The peristyle end that watched Jesse Owens, Carl Lewis, and Muhammad Ali will witness the final act of LA28. Closing ceremonies carry an emotion that opening nights can't replicate — the weight of two weeks, the friendships forged, the last time this city will glow under the rings for a generation.

Watch for Team USA making their home debut against Latvia's Nauris Miezis and the defending champs — these outdoor courts in the Valley are exactly where this sport belongs.

ARC01.blurb: The archery action kicks off with qualification rounds that aren't even ticketed — which means you're missing the most accessible way to watch Olympic archery up close. Women's compound shooters chase perfect scores while men's recurve archers establish their tournament seeding at 70 meters. Think of this as your free preview of the sport's precision before ticket prices jump for elimination rounds.

This is your crash course in archery's most technical discipline, with medals decided before 11 PM.

GAR01.blurb:
Men's qualification opens with the first subdivision — always the session that carries extra nerves as gymnasts shake off the jitters and adapt to the arena.

Zhang Boheng headlines this group as the world champion who poses Japan's biggest threat in the all-around. The 3,600-seat DTLA Arena puts you close enough to hear every landing and see every grip adjustment.

The second subdivision typically features the defending Olympic champion, and Daiki Hashimoto will deliver. Japan's gymnastics machine rarely falters in qualification, and the afternoon crowd witnesses the precision that's made Hashimoto the sport's most consistent performer for three years.

Stephen Nedoroscik could appear here — pommel horse specialists always bring theater to qualification.

Simone Biles makes her home Olympics debut. The longest session of the Games spans two full subdivisions — roughly 40 of the world's best female gymnasts, with the GOAT anchoring the drama.

If you thought the Paris crowds were loud, picture 18,000 people in DTLA Arena when Biles mounts the beam. This marathon session is where Olympic storylines are born and where the all-around, team, and apparatus finals all begin to take shape.

The other contenders in this subdivision are equally dangerous. This is where Olympic dreams take flight or crash spectacularly on a single landing.

The US women's team final is THE Olympic gymnastics event. They haven't lost this competition since Beijing 2008, and Simone Biles — if she competes — makes them nearly unbeatable.

But Rebeca Andrade leads Brazil's charge, and Italy arrives fresh off their Paris breakthrough. When 18,000 people are on their feet for every American routine, this transcends gymnastics — it becomes destiny unfolding in real time.

GAR10.blurb:
Six events, two and a half hours, one Olympic champion. Daiki Hashimoto seeks history as the first man to win three straight Olympic all-around titles since the 1960s.

Zhang Boheng didn't become world champion by accident, and the men's all-around is pure athleticism — no music, no flash, just the ultimate test of who is the most complete male gymnast alive.

potentialContenders.note: Two-time champion; projected contender for a third title

GAR11.blurb:
The most-watched event of the Olympic Games. If Simone Biles competes, this would be the final chapter of her legendary career at home — four apparatuses, two and a half hours — to determine the best female gymnast in the world.

Rebeca Andrade is the only person who consistently pushes Biles when medals are on the line. Suni Lee lurks as the defending champion who knows how to win when everything's at stake. This is the event that stops the world.

GAR12.blurb:
Apparatus finals begin with vault and pommel horse. Stephen Nedoroscik — America's most beloved gymnast after his viral Paris moment — brings his Clark Kent pommel horse routine to LA, where he could become the first American to win this event since 1984.

Women's vault features pure power: Simone Biles will likely unleash her signature Yurchenko double pike that no one else dares attempt.

potentialContenders.note: Vault goddess with moves literally named after her; has not committed to competing in LA

potentialContenders.note: Pommel horse king seeking America's first gold on event since 1984

GAR13.blurb:
Women's beam finals are the most nerve-wracking two hours in Olympic sport — four inches wide, four feet high, and careers decided by millimeters. Alice D'Amato proved in Paris that ice runs through her veins.

Simone Biles has been refining her beam routine for this exact moment. Men's parallel bars rounds out the session with upper-body artistry that showcases gymnastics at its most elegant.

GAR14.blurb:
The final individual apparatus finals feature gymnastics' two most spectacular disciplines. Women's floor combines tumbling, artistry, and pure entertainment — this is where Rebeca Andrade beat Biles in Paris and where Jordan Chiles sparked controversy.

Men's high bar closes the gymnastics program with death-defying release moves nine feet off the ground. The perfect finale to two weeks of gymnastics at LA28.

GAR15.blurb:
The mixed team event makes its Olympic debut in LA. Picture the world's best male and female gymnasts competing together for national glory in a format that could revolutionize the sport.

Nobody knows exactly what to expect, which makes it absolutely unmissable. Olympic history written in real time — and you'll be able to say you were there when gymnastics changed forever.

SWA01.blurb:
The duet technical routine is artistic swimming's precision exam — two swimmers executing required elements with no room for improvisation. Judges scrutinize every lift, spin, and vertical hold against exacting standards.

China swept Paris duet gold and arrives as the reigning World Cup champions. Spain and Japan have emerged as consistent podium threats since 2025, while the USA earned Paris medals and will lean into home crowd energy.

The Long Beach Aquatics Center puts you poolside in morning light, close enough to see the synchronization unfold. Prelims are your accessible entry point to a sport that rewards repeat viewing — the more you watch, the more you see.

Duet free routine is where artistic swimming sheds the technical rulebook and becomes pure performance art. Four minutes of choreography, music, and athleticism — two swimmers moving as one.

Medals are on the line. China dominated Paris in the absence of Russia, the sport's historical powerhouse. The USA broke a 20-year medal drought at Paris and will carry that momentum onto home pool deck.

Long Beach mid-morning: the sun overhead, the crowd leaning in. This is the session where you see the entire arc of a duet's story — technical precision meets creative risk.

Eight swimmers, one collective breath. The team technical routine is artistic swimming's ultimate synchronization test — mandatory elements performed with balletic precision by a full squad.

China shattered expectations at Paris, winning gold in Russia's absence and resetting the sport's power balance. The USA claimed silver, their first medal since 2004, and Spain took bronze. All three could return to Southern California hungry.

Prelims at Long Beach give you front-row access to the technical foundation — watch how the best teams in the world execute the required moves before the free routines unleash the creativity.

Team free routine prelims are where the gloves come off. No required elements, just four minutes of choreographed ambition — teams throw their most daring concepts into the pool and hope the judges reward the vision.

China enters as the Paris gold medalist and 2025 World Cup champion. The USA, riding their Paris silver breakthrough, will have ten thousand voices behind them in Long Beach. Spain and Japan round out the expected field.

Morning light on the pool, swimsuits flashing, music echoing off the venue. Prelims determine who advances to the final — this is where Olympic dreams either solidify or slip away.

SWA05.blurb:
The team acrobatic final is artistic swimming's gravity-defying crescendo — swimmers launch a flier two meters above the water into flips, twists, and dives, all while maintaining formation below. It's Cirque du Soleil on a liquid stage, and medals are decided here.

China threw down the gauntlet at Paris with routines that redefined what's possible. The USA matched them for creativity and earned silver in front of a home crowd at Long Beach. Spain's bronze-medal precision keeps them in the hunt.

Saturday late morning, the sun high, the stakes higher. This is the session where four years of training compresses into one final performance — you'll leave the pool talking about what you just saw.

ATH01.blurb:
The Games begin at the Coliseum with a morning of qualifying rounds across six events — sprinters open their campaigns in the 100m preliminaries, throwers stake early claims in shot put and hammer, and distance runners navigate the opening heats of the 5000m and 800m. The morning light will pour into the same stadium where Jesse Owens and Carl Lewis ran, and 68,000 fans will watch the track come alive for the first time in forty-four years.

You'll see pole vaulters clear heights in qualification — every clearance matters when three misses end your Olympics — and the raw speed of the world's fastest humans testing their lanes. Prelims are the accessible entry point to track and field: tickets are cheaper, the atmosphere is electric with first-day nerves, and you get more events in one session than any final will offer.
ATH01.potentialContendersIntro: The women's 100m field could include Paris champion Julien Alfred and silver medalist Sha'Carri Richardson, while Noah Lyles and Kishane Thompson are projected to headline the men's side if both compete.

ATH02.blurb:
The first evening session is a showcase: the women's 100m final, the women's shot put final, and the men's 10,000m final all share the same track under the lights. The 100m final will last under eleven seconds and produce the fastest woman on earth. The shot put circle sits inside the track, so you'll watch the throwers between sprint heats — every put is a chess match of technique and raw power. The 10,000m final is twenty-five laps of attrition, building to a ferocious kick finish that could decide gold by hundredths of a second.

The Coliseum crowd has a reputation — loud, knowledgeable, unforgiving of hesitation. This session mixes pure speed, endurance suffering, and the first American medal chances of the track program. If you want the full spectrum of what athletics can be, this is it.

ATH03.blurb:
Decathlon morning: the world's greatest all-around athletes open their two-day campaign with the 100m, long jump, and shot put. Every event counts, and a disaster in any discipline can end a medal bid before lunch. You'll also see the men's shot put and long jump qualifiers — Ryan Crouser could be warming up for a historic third consecutive Olympic gold, and Miltiadis Tentoglou will be hunting his third straight long jump title. The mixed 4x400m relay opens its rounds, four legs of pure chaos where one stumble or bad handoff reshapes the field.

Morning sessions at the Coliseum are quieter, more intimate — you can hear the athletes, see their faces between attempts. Decathletes are a different breed: calm under pressure, methodical, nursing energy for nine more events. If you want to watch the sport at its most complete, this is where it starts.
ATH03.potentialContendersIntro: Decathlon contenders could include Paris champion Damian Warner if he competes, while Ryan Crouser and Miltiadis Tentoglou are projected favorites in shot put and long jump qualifying.

potentialContenders.note: Two-time Olympic shot put champion chasing an unprecedented third gold

ATH04.blurb:
The men's 100m final is the centerpiece of the Games — under ten seconds of pure velocity, the title of fastest man on earth, and a roar from 68,000 people that will shake the Coliseum walls. This session also delivers the men's shot put final, where Ryan Crouser could attempt a historic three-peat, and the mixed 4x400m relay final, a brand-new Olympic format that mixes speed, strategy, and handoff drama. Decathletes continue their grind with high jump and the 400m, two events that separate contenders from pretenders.

The Coliseum has seen legendary sprint finals — Carl Lewis in '84, and now Noah Lyles and the world's fastest humans returning to the same track. The energy in the stadium during a 100m final is singular: the starter's commands, the silent tension, and then the explosion. If you attend one track session in your life, make it this one.
ATH04.potentialContendersIntro: Noah Lyles and Kishane Thompson are projected to headline the men's 100m final if both qualify, while Ryan Crouser could attempt a third consecutive Olympic shot put gold.

potentialContenders.name: Noah Lyles
potentialContenders.note: Paris 100m champion racing for a home-soil double

potentialContenders.name: Kishane Thompson
potentialContenders.note: Lost Paris 100m by 0.005 seconds — could reverse it in LA

potentialContenders.name: Ryan Crouser
potentialContenders.note: Won back-to-back Olympic golds in Tokyo and Paris, chasing history

potentialContenders.name: Jakob Ingebrigtsen
potentialContenders.note: Paris 1500m champion if he advances through round 1

ATH05.blurb:
Decathlon continues with three grueling events — 110m hurdles, discus, and pole vault — while the women's triple jump final crowns a champion. Decathletes at this stage are deep into the pain cave: managing injuries, conserving energy, calculating point spreads in their heads while clearing bars or launching implements. The triple jump final is a spectacle of explosive power — three phases of flight compressed into eight seconds, with sand spray and measurement drama determining medals.

This morning session also includes the men's 400m opening round and repechage heats for both hurdles events, giving eliminated athletes a second chance at survival. The Coliseum floor will be chaos: pole vault mats in one corner, discus throwers spinning in the infield, sprinters blasting down the straightaway. It's track and field in its purest, most overwhelming form.
ATH05.potentialContendersIntro: The women's triple jump final could feature Paris champion Yu Yaming if she competes, while decathlon contenders continue their two-day grind.

potentialContenders.note: Won Paris 2024 women's triple jump gold

potentialContenders.note: Paris decathlon champion if competing, deep into day two

potentialContenders.name: Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone
potentialContenders.note: Could appear in women's 400m hurdles repechage if needed

ATH06.blurb:
Mondo Duplantis owns the pole vault. He has won the last two Olympic golds and continues to push the world record higher — every time he competes, there's a chance you'll witness history. The men's pole vault final is pure theater: the bar rises, the field shrinks, and eventually one vaulter remains. This session also crowns the decathlon champion after ten events and two days of suffering — the 1500m is the final event, a lap of agony that determines who earns the title of World's Greatest Athlete.

The mixed 4x100m relay makes its Olympic debut in round 1 and final during this session, a brand-new event format that mixes genders and pure speed. You'll also see the men's 1500m repechage and women's 800m semifinals, both tactical battles where one misstep ends an Olympic dream. Evening light, medal stakes, and Mondo flying — this session has weight.
ATH06.potentialContendersIntro: Mondo Duplantis is the overwhelming favorite for men's pole vault gold if he competes, while the decathlon crown could come down to Paris champion Damian Warner and his rivals.

potentialContenders.name: Mondo Duplantis
potentialContenders.note: Two-time Olympic champion and world record holder in pole vault

potentialContenders.name: Damian Warner
potentialContenders.note: Paris decathlon champion finishing his two-day campaign

potentialContenders.name: Jakob Ingebrigtsen
potentialContenders.note: Paris 1500m gold medalist if appearing in repechage

ATH07.blurb:
Morning prelims across five events — pole vaulters, steeplechasers, triple jumpers, hurdlers, and 400m runners all opening their Olympic campaigns. The men's 3000m steeplechase round 1 is controlled chaos: twenty-eight barriers, seven water jumps, and a field of distance runners navigating obstacles at sub-five-minute-mile pace. The women's 400m round 1 could feature Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone if she doubles from the hurdles — a single lap of the track where the last 100 meters separate the tactical from the brave.

The Coliseum in the morning is a different animal than evening sessions: smaller crowds, more space to breathe, and a chance to watch athletes in their most vulnerable state. Qualification is binary — advance or go home — and the tension shows. You'll see relief, devastation, and everything in between.
ATH07.potentialContendersIntro: Grant Holloway is projected to headline the men's 110m hurdles rounds based on his Paris gold, while Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone could appear in the women's 400m if attempting a flat/hurdles double.

potentialContenders.note: Won Paris 110m hurdles gold with a championship record 12.89

potentialContenders.name: Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone
potentialContenders.note: World record holder in 400m hurdles, status for 400m flat unconfirmed

potentialContenders.name: Femke Bol
potentialContenders.note: Paris 400m hurdles silver and 4x400m relay gold, likely 400m contender

ATH08.blurb:
This is a medal avalanche: five finals in three hours, including the women's 5000m, women's 800m, men's high jump, and men's discus. The men's 1500m semifinals will be tactical warfare — Jakob Ingebrigtsen and Josh Kerr could meet again, reprising their Paris battle. The 400m hurdles semifinals for both genders are survival races: ten barriers, one lap, and the margin between advancing and elimination measured in hundredths. The women's 800m final is two laps of chaos, a race that explodes in the final 200 meters.

Evening sessions at the Coliseum carry a different energy than morning rounds — the crowd is louder, the stakes are clearer, and the athletes know it. You'll watch five medal ceremonies in one sitting, each with its own drama. If you want maximum return on a single ticket, this is the session.
ATH08.potentialContendersIntro: Jakob Ingebrigtsen and Josh Kerr are projected to clash again in the men's 1500m semifinals if both advance, while Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone could dominate the women's 400m hurdles semifinal.

potentialContenders.note: Paris 1500m champion, tactical genius of middle distance

potentialContenders.note: Paris 1500m silver medalist with the kick to beat anyone

potentialContenders.note: Paris 200m champion likely appearing in opening rounds

ATH09.blurb:
The women's 3000m steeplechase final is the centerpiece — seven and a half laps of barriers and water jumps, a race that rewards both speed and fearlessness. Steeplechase finals are unpredictable: one stumble at the water barrier can end a medal bid, and the final lap often reshapes the entire field. This session also features repechage rounds for the 400m and 200m, giving eliminated athletes a second chance at survival, and men's hammer qualification, where throwers spin massive weights inside a safety cage.

Morning sessions are cheaper entry points to the Games, and this one delivers a gold-medal race plus the chaos of second-chance heats. The women's long jump qualification runs concurrently — you'll see athletes measuring their run-ups, testing the pit, and hunting one clean jump to advance. It's track and field in its most raw, unfiltered form.
ATH09.potentialContendersIntro: The women's 3000m steeplechase final could feature Paris medalists Beatrice Chebet, Jem Cain, and Faith Kipyegon if they compete.

potentialContenders.note: Won Paris 2024 women's 3000m steeplechase gold

potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 women's 3000m steeplechase silver medalist

potentialContenders.name: Faith Kipyegon
potentialContenders.note: Paris 2024 women's 3000m steeplechase bronze, two-time 1500m champion

ATH10.blurb:
The men's 1500m final is one of the greatest Olympic races — tactical, dramatic, and historically defined by the final 200-meter kick. Jakob Ingebrigtsen and Josh Kerr could clash again, reprising their Paris battle on American soil. The metric mile rewards both patience and explosive speed; the winner is often decided in the final fifty meters. This session also delivers the men's long jump final, where Miltiadis Tentoglou could attempt a third consecutive Olympic gold, and the men's 400m hurdles final, ten barriers of pain and precision.

You'll also see the women's discus final and early rounds of the women's 200m, mixing medal stakes with preliminary heats. Evening sessions at the Coliseum are electric — the crowd knows what's at stake, and the athletes feed off it. The 1500m final alone is worth the price of admission.
ATH10.potentialContendersIntro: Jakob Ingebrigtsen and Josh Kerr are projected to headline the men's 1500m final if both advance, while Miltiadis Tentoglou could chase a third straight Olympic long jump gold.

potentialContenders.note: Paris 1500m champion, tactical genius with devastating kick

potentialContenders.name: Josh Kerr
potentialContenders.note: Paris 1500m silver medalist who has beaten Ingebrigtsen before

potentialContenders.name: Miltiadis Tentoglou
potentialContenders.note: Two-time Olympic long jump champion chasing history

ATH11.blurb:
The men's 3000m steeplechase final is eight laps of endurance, obstacles, and water barrier drama — a race that rewards both speed and the ability to hurdle cleanly while fatigued. Steeplechase finals are unpredictable and brutal, often decided in the final lap when legs are heavy and form collapses. This session also features early rounds for the men's 800m and women's 200m, plus qualification in the women's javelin and high jump — two field events where every attempt matters.

Morning sessions are quieter, more focused — you can hear the steeplechasers breathing as they approach the water jump, see the javelin throwers measuring their run-ups. The men's 3000m steeplechase final will crown a champion, and the qualifying rounds will determine who survives to compete another day. If you want track and field without the evening-session crowds, this is your window.
ATH11.potentialContendersIntro: The men's 3000m steeplechase final could feature Paris champion Simon Koech if he competes, while the men's 800m rounds may include world record holder Emmanuel Wanyonyi.

potentialContenders.name: Simon Koech

ATH12.blurb:
The women's 400m hurdles final is the marquee event — Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone versus Femke Bol, world record holder versus silver medalist, one lap of ten barriers and tactical warfare. McLaughlin-Levrone has owned this event, but Bol is relentless and calculating. This session also delivers the men's hammer final, where athletes spin massive weights inside a safety cage, and semifinals in the men's 110m hurdles and 200m — survival rounds where lane assignments and hundredths of a second determine who advances.

You'll also see the women's pole vault final, where athletes clear heights until one remains, and the women's and men's 400m semifinals. Evening sessions at the Coliseum are electric, and this one mixes speed, power, and endurance across six events. The 400m hurdles final alone could be the race of the Games.

ATH13.blurb:
Heptathlon begins — the ultimate all-around test for female athletes. Seven events over two days determine the best on earth, and day one opens with the 100m hurdles and high jump. Every clearance, every tenth of a second, every misstep compounds across six more events. This session also delivers the men's triple jump final, where athletes compress explosive power into three phases of flight, and early rounds in the men's 5000m and women's 100m hurdles.

Morning sessions are intimate — you can see the heptathletes calculating point spreads between events, managing energy, nursing injuries. The triple jump final is pure spectacle: long run-ups, sand spray, and measurement drama. If you want to watch the sport at its most complete and unforgiving, this is where it starts.
ATH13.potentialContendersIntro: The women's heptathlon could feature Paris silver medalist Anna Hall if she competes, while the men's triple jump final may include Paris champion Pedro Pichardo.

potentialContenders.note: Won Paris 2024 heptathlon silver with 6,804 points

potentialContenders.note: Won Paris 2024 men's triple jump gold with 17.87m

ATH14.blurb:
The men's 200m final is pure speed around the bend — Noah Lyles versus Letsile Tebogo, defending champions in the 100m and 200m respectively, could meet again on American soil. The 200m rewards bend-running skill and raw velocity; evening conditions often produce the fastest times. This session also crowns champions in the women's 10,000m and men's 110m hurdles, plus semifinals in the women's 1500m and 200m. Heptathletes continue their grind with shot put and 200m.

Evening sessions at the Coliseum carry maximum energy — the crowd knows what's at stake, and the athletes feed off it. The men's 200m final and women's 10,000m final are both flagship events, mixing pure speed with endurance suffering. You'll see three gold medals awarded in one sitting, plus the heptathlon leaderboard shifting with every throw and sprint.

ATH15.blurb:
Heptathlon concludes with three events — long jump, javelin, and the 800m final — determining the World's Greatest Female Athlete. The 800m is the final event, two laps of agony where accumulated fatigue from six prior events separates champions from contenders. This session also delivers four individual finals: women's long jump, women's 400m, men's 400m, and women's 200m. You'll watch five gold medals awarded in four hours, plus the men's 4x100m relay opening rounds.

The Coliseum will be loud — home crowd, medal stakes, and championship-level performances across every event. The women's 400m final could feature Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone if she doubles, and the men's 400m final is a single-lap test of speed and pain tolerance. If you want maximum medal density in one session, this is it.
ATH15.potentialContendersIntro: Anna Hall could be chasing heptathlon gold if she competes, while Gabby Thomas is projected to defend her Paris 200m title and Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone may double into the 400m flat.

ATH16.blurb:
This Sunday evening session at the Coliseum packs eight events into a relentless two-and-a-half-hour block, anchored by the men's 4×100m relay final — four legs of sub-10 speed and baton drama that will shake the peristyle arches. You'll also witness Faith Kipyegon's bid for a historic third straight 1500m gold, the men's 800m final exploding over its final furious 200 meters, and the women's high jump final where the bar keeps rising until one athlete remains. Relay rounds for both 4×100m and 4×400m teams set the stage for Monday's finals, while the women's 100m hurdles semifinals determine who advances to the medal race.

The LA Memorial Coliseum holds 78,000 and has hosted two prior Olympics — this will be its third, a first in Olympic history. Carl Lewis won four golds here in 1984; Jesse Owens's legacy haunts the same infield. Evening light, a roaring home crowd, and the signature events of track and field all compressed into one session make this among the most electric tickets of the Games.
ATH16.potentialContendersIntro: The men's 4×100m relay could feature Noah Lyles anchoring for Team USA, who took silver at Paris 2024. Faith Kipyegon of Kenya is the two-time defending 1500m champion and heavily favored for a three-peat if she competes.

ATH17.blurb:
The final night of track at the Coliseum delivers six straight finals in under two hours — a fitting crescendo for the Games' most storied venue. The men's 5000m final could see Jakob Ingebrigtsen of Norway defend his Paris title against a surging American distance squad led by Paris bronze medalist Grant Fisher, while the women's 100m hurdles final pits reigning champion Masai Russell against the world's fastest technical sprinters. Both 4×100m and 4×400m relay finals for men and women close the session with pure kinetic chaos — batons flying, crowd noise peaking, and medal races decided by hundredths of a second.

This is the Coliseum at full throat: 78,000 fans, floodlights ablaze, and relay teams chasing gold in the same stadium where Carl Lewis anchored USA to 4×100m gold in 1984. The men's javelin final adds a field event punctuation mark, and if USA's women take the 4×100m podium with Sha'Carri Richardson in the mix, the decibel level could rival a USC game day. Evening sessions here are a ritual — plan to stay for the whole block and soak in the atmosphere.
ATH17.potentialContendersIntro: The men's 5000m final is projected to feature Jakob Ingebrigtsen, the Paris 2024 champion, and Grant Fisher, who won bronze in Paris. USA's relay squads are expected contenders after Paris golds in both 4×400m races and silver in the men's 4×100m.

ATH18.blurb:
Race walking is the Olympics' most technically demanding endurance event — one foot must always touch the ground, both knees must stay straight, and three red cards means disqualification. The half-marathon distance tests tactical intelligence as much as fitness.

Spain's Alvaro Martin and Ecuador's Brian Daniel Pintado have been trading world titles, while Yang Jiayu makes the discipline look effortless. The race finishes inside the Coliseum — the same dramatic entrance that marathon runners have used since 1984.

ATH19.blurb:
Venice Beach at sunrise. The world's fastest women tackle 26.2 miles along the California coast — and it's free to watch from the roadside.

Tigst Assefa's world record of 2:11:53 has redefined what's possible in the women's marathon. Sifan Hassan proved in Paris that distance running's boldest athletes can do things nobody imagined. The early start means cool ocean air, golden morning light, and a finish inside the Coliseum where Olympic legends are made.

BDM01.blurb: Your first taste of Olympic badminton — and the fastest racquet sport in the world — starts at the crack of dawn in the Galen Center. Watch Viktor Axelsen warm up those thunderous smashes alongside defending mixed doubles champions Zheng Si Wei and Huang Ya Qiong. Group play is where upsets happen and careers are made, plus you'll catch multiple courts in action.

BSB01.blurb:
Baseball returns to the Olympics after a 20-year absence with opening-round pool play at Dodger Stadium. The host USA faces off against a field that could include the Dominican Republic, Venezuela, and Japan in the tournament's first games.

MLB and the Players Association are in active discussions about releasing active players for the Games. If a deal is reached, this becomes the most talent-rich Olympic baseball tournament in history.

Morning baseball in Chavez Ravine — the San Gabriel Mountains frame the outfield, and you'll watch world-class pitching and power under the Southern California sun.
BSB01.potentialContendersIntro: The field depends on MLB-MLBPA negotiations, but USA, Dominican Republic, and Venezuela have already qualified through World Baseball Classic results.

potentialContenders.note: Qualified via 2026 World Baseball Classic; Ronald Acuna Jr. could anchor the lineup if healthy and released by MLB

potentialContenders.note: Likely qualifier; reigning Olympic gold medalists from Tokyo 2020, with NPB expected to pause for LA28

The stakes for these prelims: finishing atop your three-team group means a direct pass to the semis. Second and third place face a win-or-go-home quarterfinal.

Night games at Dodger Stadium are iconic — the stadium lights up, the city glows beyond the outfield pavilions, and the atmosphere builds as the desert air cools.

BSB04.blurb:
The final Saturday evening of pool play could determine which teams earn a direct semifinal berth. With two games already played, every pitch carries playoff weight.

A Japan-USA matchup at this stage would pit reigning Olympic champions against the host nation — Dodgers teammates Ohtani and Yamamoto on opposite sides, if MLB players compete. Dominican Republic-Venezuela would be a Caribbean baseball rivalry on the Olympic stage.

The weekend crowd at Dodger Stadium brings energy — this is LA baseball culture, transplanted fans repping their countries, and the stands loud for every strikeout.

BSB06.blurb:
The closing games of round-robin play under the lights. Pool standings finalize tonight — one team per group advances directly to the semis, the rest play Monday's quarterfinals.

A late-inning rally here could mean the difference between a day off and a do-or-die knockout game. Closers are warming, managers are managing pitch counts, and every defensive play matters.

Sunday night at Dodger Stadium has a different feel than the weekend day games — louder, sharper, the stakes visible in every at-bat.

Win-or-go-home baseball. The two play-in quarterfinals pit the second- and third-place pool finishers against each other — lose and your Olympics are over.

This is where tournament pressure arrives. Managers burn their best relievers, position players dig in for nine-inning battles, and a single swing can end a nation's medal hopes.

Monday afternoon at Dodger Stadium — the casual weekend vibe is gone, replaced by the taut intensity of elimination baseball.

A Japan-Venezuela matchup could feature Ohtani vs. Acuna — two transcendent talents on the Olympic stage. USA-Dominican Republic would be Judge vs. Soto, power vs. pure hitting, if MLB releases players. Whoever wins plays for gold in 48 hours.

Olympic gold medal baseball at Dodger Stadium. If MLB and the Players Association finalize a release deal, this is Ohtani, Judge, Soto, Acuna — the best players on earth, under the lights, for a gold medal.

BOX18.blurb:
The first gold medal of LA boxing — women's 70kg — gets decided inside DTLA Arena's electric 3,600-seat cauldron. Three semifinals also set up the weekend's championship bouts.

This venue is the point. While other sports fill massive arenas, boxing's Olympic finals happen in a room where you hear every jab echo off the walls. The intimacy transforms every combination into pure theater.

BOX23.blurb:
Four gold medals in one session — Olympic champions crowned every 30 minutes for two hours straight. From featherweight precision to middleweight power, DTLA Arena becomes the center of the boxing universe.

This is the first Olympics with full gender parity in boxing — seven weight classes each for men and women — so two of tonight's four finals are women's bouts. While basketball fills 18,000-seat venues and soccer packs 90,000 into the Rose Bowl, boxing's championship night puts you close enough to hear the leather land.

The intimacy is the point. The compressed energy of a tiny room, the national pride, the drama of two people fighting for the ultimate prize in sport — and when it's over, you walk out into the DTLA night.

The solo time trials separate the seamanship legends from the flatwater specialists, setting up knockout rounds where one rogue wave can end Olympic dreams.

potentialContenders.note: The original skateboarding prodigy seeking redemption

SKB02.blurb: Nyjah Huston returns to Venice Beach — the spot where he first learned to kickflip — carrying the weight of American skateboarding on his shoulders. The prelims are where legends are made and dreams are shattered; one bad slam or missed trick sends you home empty-handed while the world watches. This isn't just qualification rounds — it's skateboarding's most loaded field ever assembled in the sport's birthplace.

potentialContenders.name: Nyjah Huston
potentialContenders.note: Paris gold medalist competing in his actual backyard

potentialContenders.name: Yuto Horigome
potentialContenders.note: Tokyo champion with unmatched technical precision

potentialContenders.note: Tokyo bronze medalist with home crowd advantage

SKB03.blurb: Eight women, three runs, five tricks — Olympic street skateboarding gold gets decided where it all began. Coco Yoshizawa faces the ultimate test: defending her Paris crown in front of skateboarding's most knowledgeable crowd at Venice Beach, where every rail and ledge has been sessioned by legends. When the pressure peaks and the tricks get gnarlier, this is skateboarding's purest Olympic moment.

potentialContenders.name: Coco Yoshizawa
potentialContenders.note: Defending champion with ice-cold composure under pressure

potentialContenders.name: Poe Pinson
potentialContenders.note: American rising star with massive tricks and home advantage

potentialContenders.note: Technical wizard who never misses when it counts

SKB04.blurb:
Nyjah Huston defending his Paris gold at Venice Beach — his literal home turf where he learned to skate — is the ultimate LA 2028 storyline. The world's best street skaters throwing down technical bangers on stairs, rails, and ledges with the Pacific Ocean as a backdrop?

This is skateboarding's Super Bowl. Yuto Horigome brings the most refined technical game in the world, while the young guns are hungry to dethrone the kings on skating's most sacred ground.

potentialContenders.name: Nyjah Huston
potentialContenders.note: Paris gold medalist competing at his home spot — doesn't get more perfect than that.

SKB05.blurb: This is where you witness park skating's raw artistry before the pressure cooker of finals — flowing runs through deep bowls just minutes from where Tony Hawk and the Z-Boys revolutionized the sport. Watch Tom Schaar drop in at his home break, plus international heavyweights testing their most ambitious lines when the stakes are still manageable. You're getting front-row seats to skateboarding's most creative discipline without the finals crowd.

SKB06.blurb: The teenage takeover begins here — women's park is dominated by skaters who can't legally drive, led by 14-year-old Paris champion Arisa Trew attempting tricks that defy physics. Sky Brown, the grizzled veteran at 18, faces a new generation of fearless kids who grew up watching her Olympic runs. This prelim session is your chance to see the future of skateboarding before they become household names.

SKB07.blurb: The men's park final is skateboarding's most artistic battleground — three runs to prove who can best blend technical wizardry with creative flow through Venice's purpose-built bowls. Keegan Palmer defends his Tokyo crown against hometown hero Tom Schaar, while Brazil's Pedro Barros brings two Olympic medals worth of experience to this concrete canvas. This is THE session where park skating's poetry meets its athleticism.

SKB08.blurb: Arisa Trew attempts to defend her Paris gold at just 16 — an eternity in women's park where careers peak in the early teens. Sky Brown, now the elder stateswoman, brings Olympic experience against a field of kids who learned kickturns before algebra. Three runs, nine minutes of pure artistry, and skateboarding's most unpredictable discipline crowning its queen just steps from the Pacific.

SQU01.blurb: Squash makes its Olympic debut after decades of lobbying — and The glass courts at the purpose-built Comcast Squash Center put you inside the action like no other Olympic venue. Watch Ali Farag and Nour El Sherbini's Egyptian squad try to dominate from day one.

SQU02.blurb: This is your best shot to see squash's lightning-fast rallies. Egypt's depth is staggering — they could sweep multiple medals — but dark horses from Malaysia and New Zealand love playing spoiler. The glass court setup means every seat is courtside.

SQU03.blurb: Round of 16 action continues with the tournament's most intriguing matchups taking shape. This is where early upsets happen — and where squash's global reach really shows, from Hong Kong to Colombia. For a sport making its Olympic bow, these early rounds have genuine upset potential.

SQU04.blurb: The final Round of 16 session before squash gets serious — and your last chance to catch this sport at entry-level prices. Egypt's conveyor belt of talent will be on full display, but History starts here.

SQU05.blurb: Quarterfinal night one — where medal hopes get crushed against Comcast's glass walls. Egypt could feasibly have six players across both draws reaching this stage, but upsets in the Round of 16 make every match unpredictable. The tournament's true contenders separate themselves here.

SQU06.blurb: The quarterfinal gauntlet concludes with medal positions on the line. This is where Ali Farag's championship experience meets the pressure of inaugurating Olympic squash — and where upsets could reshape the entire medal picture. Four semifinal spots, eight desperate players.

SQU07.blurb: Women's semifinal night — where Olympic squash history gets written. Nouran Gohar and Nour El Sherbini could set up an all-Egyptian final, but Amanda Sobhy has the home crowd and nothing to lose. Two matches, four women, and the sport's inaugural Olympic medals hanging in the balance.

SQU08.blurb: Men's semifinal drama at its finest — Ali Farag's quest for squash immortality hits its crucial stage. Mohamed ElShorbagy switched countries for this exact moment, while dark horses like Paul Coll could crash Egypt's gold party. The glass court will be electric with Olympic tension.

SQU09.blurb:
The first Olympic squash gold medal EVER gets decided tonight. If Egypt's Nouran Gohar and Nour El Sherbini both advance, we get a dream final between current and former world No. 1s.

If Amanda Sobhy crashes the party, the Comcast Center roof might blow off. Pure sporting history in a glass box.

SQU10.blurb:
Ali Farag's coronation as squash's first Olympic champion? Or the upset that launches this sport into mainstream consciousness?

After decades of Olympic exclusion, squash's inaugural men's gold medal carries the weight of the sport's entire future. This is THE moment squash has been waiting for since 1998.

SRF01.blurb: This is where the entire field gets their first taste of Trestles under Olympic pressure — and where dark horses like local hero Caitlin Simmers could announce themselves to the world. The beauty of Round 1 is watching Gabriel Medina and John John Florence surf alongside complete unknowns on the same sacred break.

SRF02.blurb: Round 2 at Trestles is where Olympic dreams get real — half the field goes home, half advances to glory. Caroline Marks will be feeling the pressure of defending gold on waves she's surfed thousands of times, while Filipe Toledo's high-performance style should shine on Trestles' technical walls. The morning glass-off conditions here are legendary, so the early heats could produce magic.

SRF03.blurb: The field narrows to eight per gender, and Trestles will separate the technicians from the pretenders. John John Florence's natural flow should be on full display as he hunts his first Olympic medal, while Brazil's depth means someone like Medina or Toledo will likely punch through. This is your last chance to see Olympic surfing at Trestles for .

SRF04.blurb: This is it — Olympic gold decided at surfing's most sacred venue. Caroline Marks defending her title at Trestles would be pure California storybook, while John John Florence finally claiming Olympic glory on his sport's holy grail wave would cement his legend. From dawn patrol quarterfinals to sunset medal ceremonies, this is THE day that crowns surfing's Olympic royalty.

SWM01.blurb: This morning prelim session at the Rose Bowl Aquatics Center kicks off LA28 swimming with heats in women's 100m butterfly, men's 400m freestyle, men's 50m butterfly, women's 50m backstroke, men's 100m breaststroke, women's 400m freestyle, and mixed/women's 4x100m freestyle relays, where swimmers vie for semifinal spots in an eight-lane outdoor pool under the Pasadena sky. With every seat close to the water, prelims offer an accessible entry point to the competition at prices from $50 to over $1,100, showcasing a broad field from top seeds to national qualifiers. The format advances the fastest times, setting the stakes early in the nine-day program.

SWM03.blurb: This preliminary session opens the eight-night swimming program at the brand-new Rose Bowl Aquatics Center with four heats across four distinct events: women's 100m breaststroke, men's 100m backstroke, women's 100m freestyle, and men's 400m individual medley. Prelims are the gateway to the medal rounds—swimmers must post fast enough times to advance through semifinals or directly to finals, making every split and turn consequential. The outdoor venue under the Pasadena sky offers an accessible entry point to see the sport's depth, from established names to emerging competitors racing for their Olympic moment.

SWM05.blurb: This morning prelim session at the Rose Bowl Aquatics Center features six events across multiple distances and strokes, showcasing the breadth of Olympic swimming. Heats for the men's 1500m freestyle—the longest individual race in the pool—run alongside finals-day preliminaries for the women's 400m IM, men's 200m freestyle, women's 100m backstroke, and men's 200m butterfly. Prelim sessions offer an accessible entry point to see elite swimmers compete in the cool morning air under Pasadena's open sky, with every seat close to the water at this purpose-built venue.

SWM15.blurb: This morning prelim session at the Rose Bowl Aquatics Center features heats for the women's 50m freestyle, 200m butterfly, 800m freestyle, and both men's and women's 4x100m medley relays, determining who advances to the evening finals. With up to eight heats per event, spectators witness a broad field of international swimmers jostling for top times under the Pasadena sky, all from seats close to the water's edge. At an accessible price point starting around $50, it's an ideal session to experience the full depth of Olympic swimming competition.
SWM15.potentialContendersIntro: Projected contenders in these prelims include sprint and distance specialists from recent world rankings and Paris 2024 results, though LA28 participation remains unconfirmed for all athletes.

SWM16.blurb: This evening session at the Rose Bowl Aquatics Center decides gold medals in the men's 100m butterfly, men's 50m backstroke, women's 50m breaststroke, men's 800m freestyle, and 4x100m mixed medley relay, alongside semifinals in the women's 50m freestyle and women's 200m butterfly. Swimmers launch from blocks into the outdoor pool under Pasadena's night sky, with every seat offering a close view of the action in this brand-new venue adjacent to the Rose Bowl. The mixed medley relay final caps the night with teams racing all four strokes in a high-stakes team battle.
SWM16.potentialContendersIntro: Projected contenders in these events draw from Paris 2024 standouts, though no LA28 participation is confirmed. Likely fields feature sprint specialists and versatile swimmers from the US, Australia, China, and Europe.

SWM17.blurb: This session closes the LA28 swimming program at the brand-new Rose Bowl Aquatics Center in Pasadena, awarding gold medals in the women's 50m freestyle final—a blistering one-lap sprint—women's 200m butterfly final, women's 800m freestyle final, men's 4x100m medley relay final, and women's 4x100m medley relay final under the evening sky. Each relay features four swimmers tackling backstroke, breaststroke, butterfly, and freestyle in sequence, delivering high-stakes team racing with perfect starts and seamless transitions deciding the podium. Expect an electric atmosphere as the crowd roars for the final night of finals, with every seat offering close-up views of the action.
SWM17.potentialContendersIntro: Projected contenders in this session draw from Paris 2024 standouts in sprint freestyle, butterfly, distance freestyle, and medley relays, though no LA28 participation has been confirmed. Nations like the USA, Australia, and Canada are likely to field strong relay squads alongside individual stars.

TTE01.blurb:
This is your ticket to witness table tennis wizardry up close. Opening day features Fan Zhendong and Wang Chuqin navigating early rounds alongside mixed doubles action, where chemistry matters as much as reflexes.

The beauty of Round of 64 sessions? You'll see China's superstars forced to play their A-game from minute one, because even world #1 players can lose to a hot opponent with nothing to lose.

potentialContenders.name: Fan Zhendong
potentialContenders.note: Paris gold medalist who never takes early rounds lightly

potentialContenders.name: Wang Chuqin
potentialContenders.note: Fan's biggest rival and mixed doubles specialist

potentialContenders.name: Tomokazu Harimoto
potentialContenders.note: Japan's best shot at ending China's dominance

potentialContenders.name: Truls Möregårdh
potentialContenders.note: Left-handed trickster who can trouble anyone on his day

TTE02.blurb: Doubles table tennis is chess at 100 mph, and this afternoon session showcases the format at its finest. Watch how the Chinese pairs communicate through subtle glances and positioning — it's like they share a tennis brain. The Round of 16 is where partnerships either click perfectly or crumble under Olympic pressure.

potentialContenders.name: Wang Chuqin/Fan Zhendong
potentialContenders.note: Dynamic duo combining power and finesse

potentialContenders.name: Chen Meng/Wang Manyu
potentialContenders.note: Women's doubles powerhouse with devastating forehand combinations

potentialContenders.name: Lim Jonghoon/Jang Woojin
potentialContenders.note: South Korea's disciplined pair with upset potential

TTE03.blurb: Prime time table tennis means Chen Meng and Sun Yingsha enter the fray in what could be your best value ticket of the Games. These evening prelims let you study the legend-making strokes of China's women's brigade without paying finals prices. Plus, there's something magical about the crack of plastic on paddle echoing through the Convention Center after dark.

potentialContenders.name: Chen Meng
potentialContenders.note: Two-time Olympic gold medalist with ice-cold composure

potentialContenders.name: Sun Yingsha
potentialContenders.note: Aggressive attacking style that electrifies crowds

potentialContenders.name: Hina Hayata
potentialContenders.note: Japan's top woman with dreams of toppling China

potentialContenders.name: Adriana Díez
potentialContenders.note: Spain's crafty veteran who could spring a surprise

TTE04.blurb: Mixed doubles quarterfinals are where Olympic dreams collide with partnership chemistry. Wang Chuqin and Sun Yingsha form China's most lethal combination, but mixed doubles has produced more upsets than any other table tennis format. One bad game from either partner and the world's best pairs go home empty-handed.

potentialContenders.name: Wang Chuqin/Sun Yingsha
potentialContenders.note: China's dream team with complementary playing styles

TTE05.blurb: Sunday afternoon singles rounds are perfect for discovering tomorrow's table tennis stars before they become household names. This session wraps up the Round of 64 carnage, where ranked players face their first real test and dark horses announce themselves. It's affordable scouting for the sport's future legends.

TTE06.blurb: Mixed doubles semifinals under the lights — this is when table tennis becomes pure theater. The Convention Center acoustics will amplify every grunt, every paddle scrape, every crowd gasp as two teams fight for Tuesday's gold medal match. China's dominance means nothing if they choke here, and their opponents know it.

potentialContenders.name: Wang Chuqin/Sun Yingsha
potentialContenders.note: Overwhelming favorites but pressure can crack anyone

potentialContenders.name: Tomokazu Harimoto/Hina Hayata

TTE07.blurb: Doubles quarterfinals are where table tennis partnerships prove their worth or die trying. The Chinese machine faces its first real test as non-Chinese pairs start believing they can actually win medals. This Monday morning session could reshape the entire doubles landscape if upsets start flying.

TTE09.blurb: The first table tennis gold of LA 2028 gets decided here, and mixed doubles finals are always unpredictable action. Wang Chuqin and Sun Yingsha enter as overwhelming favorites, but this format has produced more stunning upsets than any other in Olympic table tennis history. When partnerships crumble under pressure, legends are born.

Fan Zhendong and Wang Chuqin will be in doubles action while singles players fight for Round of 16 spots. The intensity ratchets up as the field narrows and medal hopes crystallize.

This is THE session to witness Chinese table tennis perfection, or the stunning upset that breaks their decade-long doubles dominance. Either way, you're watching history.

Chen Meng and Wang Manyu represent the final piece of China's potential sweep, but doubles has a way of humbling even the greatest players. This evening finale could cap off Chinese dominance or witness a stunning upset that changes everything.

The Round of 16 is deceptively crucial: one bad game and Olympic dreams evaporate in seconds.

Chen Meng will be hunting her third consecutive Olympic gold, starting here with the methodical precision that's made her untouchable. Expect at least one shocking upset that'll have the table tennis world buzzing by dinner.

This session wraps up the Round of 16 with the kind of intensity that makes you forget you're watching preliminaries. This is historically when non-Chinese players find their breakthrough moments, riding adrenaline and crowd energy to upset victories. Chen Meng's quest for three straight golds faces its first serious test here.

Hall 3's acoustics will amplify every rally in what promises to be an instant classic, with the bronze medal match serving as the perfect appetizer.

These are the four best male players on the planet, and their tactical chess matches will be dissected by coaches for years. Olympic history gets written in afternoon semifinals.

These two Chinese masters have split their recent encounters, making this the most anticipated match in the sport since Ma Long's retirement. The Convention Center will shake with every winning shot in what could be the tournament's most spectacular final.

China's mixed team dominance faces its stiffest challenge from Japan's balanced squad, while surprise packages like South Korea could shake up the group standings. Five hours of round-robin action where every point shapes the knockout bracket.

WLF01.blurb: The men's 65kg class is where technical precision meets explosive power — these lifters move nearly three times their body weight with ballet-like grace. Watch for the classic battle between snatch specialists and clean-and-jerk monsters.

This weight class consistently produces the most dramatic comebacks when lifters chase Olympic qualifying totals.

WRE01.blurb: Wrestling returns to LA for the first time since 1984, and you're watching history unfold on the mats. Yui Susaki could contend for a third Olympic gold at 50kg — she's lost exactly once in her entire career, making this as close to a foregone conclusion as Olympic sport gets. The Greco-Roman heavyweights at 130kg provide the raw power spectacle, while 60kg delivers pure technical wizardry.
